</imports>

<insights>

- 23 entry points — wide API surface, consider consolidating exports
- At 47 files, this zone is large but maintains acceptable cohesion (0.51); its 23 entry points signal it is a genuine shared library rather than a monolithic blob.
- The zone receives heavy inbound coupling from unit-analyze (22 imports) and cli (38 imports), confirming its role as the foundational domain API — this is expected and healthy.
- Consider grouping the many removal operations (remove-epic, remove-feature, remove-task, delete) under a sub-namespace to clarify intent and reduce cognitive load for new contributors.
- 23 entry points in a 47-file zone is high; publishing a narrower public surface via public.ts and deprecating direct sub-path imports would reduce accidental coupling.
- Core and cli have bidirectional imports (core → cli: 15, cli → core: 38), creating a soft circular dependency risk at the zone boundary that should be resolved by ensuring cli never re-exports into core.
- The 50 outgoing calls to prd-analysis-engine from prd-domain-operations suggest domain operations depend on analysis results at runtime — this inversion (domain depending on analysis) should be reviewed; domain logic should typically be analysis-agnostic.
- prd-domain-operations → prd-analysis-engine has 50 call-graph edges; domain-layer code depending on analysis-layer output is an inversion of the expected dependency direction and risks coupling stable domain logic to volatile analysis heuristics.
- prd-domain-operations has the highest internal call volume (1591) of any zone in the batch, and outgoing calls represent only 4.6% of total calls (77 of 1668 total). It is structurally the closest zone to a stable dependency foundation — the sole obstacle is the 50-call outgoing dependency on prd-analysis-engine. Severing that one dependency corridor would promote this zone to a true stable sink.
- The import-cohesion (0.51) vs call-graph cohesion (0.95) gap of 0.44 indicates the zone is functionally tight at runtime; its many entry points inflate the import-based coupling score without reflecting actual call-time fragmentation.
- The 50 outgoing call-graph edges from prd-domain-operations to prd-analysis-engine mean that stable domain logic depends on volatile analysis heuristics at the function-call level, not just the import level. A format change in any analysis output type propagates directly into domain operation logic, inverting the stability hierarchy. Introducing a typed result interface owned by domain operations and implemented by analysis would break this dependency direction.
- src/core/analytics.ts is classified in prd-domain-operations while src/core/token-usage.ts is classified in token-usage-analytics. Both files live under src/core/ and are analytically related, creating a directory-zone mismatch: a developer browsing src/core/ encounters files belonging to two different zones with no visual boundary between them. This violates the principle of least surprise for directory navigation.
- At 47 files and 23 entry points, prd-domain-operations has no observable public.ts gate — the CLAUDE.md contract (src/public.ts as the sole public surface) appears unenforced here. Without a public surface gate, the 23 entry points grow organically and the 128 incoming call-graph edges attach to arbitrary internal files rather than a declared API.
- src/core/analytics.ts belongs to a different zone than src/core/token-usage.ts despite sharing the same directory and conceptual domain. Either relocate analytics.ts into token-usage-analytics or explicitly document in CLAUDE.md that analytics.ts is a general-purpose utility distinct from token-usage calculations to prevent future misclassification.
- 23 entry points with 128 incoming call-graph edges and no visible public.ts gate means callers attach to arbitrary internal files. Enforce the src/public.ts contract by adding a CI lint rule that rejects cross-zone imports not routed through public.ts — this converts 128 uncontrolled attachment points into a single versioned surface.
- [call graph] 1591 internal calls, 77 outgoing, 128 incoming (cohesion: 0.95, coupling: 0.05)

</insights>
